questions.prompt: What is the transfer function of the first-order example in the note?
questions.explanation: The note introduces the first-order system exactly in the form H(s)=K/(s+B) with K>0 and B>0.

questions.prompt: Where is the pole of H(s)=K/(s+B)?
questions.explanation: The denominator becomes zero when s=-B, so the single pole lies on the negative real axis.

questions.prompt: What is H(omega) after substituting s=j omega?
questions.explanation: The frequency response is found by replacing s with j omega, giving H(omega)=K/(j omega + B).

questions.prompt: What is the magnitude response of the first-order system?
questions.explanation: The complex denominator magnitude is sqrt(omega^2 + B^2), so |H(omega)| becomes K divided by that quantity.

questions.prompt: What is the phase response of the first-order system?
questions.explanation: Because the denominator angle is tan^-1(omega/B), the overall phase of the reciprocal is its negative.

questions.prompt: What happens to |H(omega)| as omega approaches 0?
questions.explanation: At very low frequency the omega term disappears, leaving the magnitude close to K/B.

questions.prompt: What happens to the phase as omega approaches infinity?
questions.explanation: As omega/B becomes very large, tan^-1(omega/B) tends to 90 degrees, so the negative phase tends to -90 degrees.
Substituting omega=B gives sqrt(B^2+B^2)=sqrt(2)B in the denominator, so the magnitude becomes K/(sqrt(2)B).

questions.prompt: Why is B called the 3 dB bandwidth in this note?
options: Because the magnitude at omega=B is 3 dB below the low-frequency value
Because the phase becomes -3 degrees there
Because the pole is located at -3B
questions.correctAnswer: Because the magnitude at omega=B is 3 dB below the low-frequency value
questions.explanation: The note shows that the magnitude at omega=B is reduced by a factor of 1/sqrt(2), which corresponds to a drop of 3 dB.

questions.prompt: How does the note classify the first-order response H(s)=2/(s+2)?
questions.explanation: The magnitude is strongest at low frequencies and decays as frequency increases, which is exactly the low-pass behavior described in the note.
